title: Dog Clear Tearing and Brown Staining: Causes and Veterinary Care
meta_desc: Noticed clear tears and brown stains on your dog? Learn about epiphora, why it happens, and when you should consult a veterinarian for care.
content_html:
What is Clear Tearing and Brown Staining?

Clear tearing followed by reddish-brown staining is common in many dog breeds, particularly those with lighter fur. This condition is known as epiphora. The brown color comes from porphyrins, which are iron-containing molecules excreted in tears, saliva, and urine. When these tears sit on the fur, the iron oxidizes upon exposure to air, creating the characteristic rust-colored stain.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for clear tearing with brown staining is generally Low. While the staining can be unsightly, it is often a chronic condition related to face shape, allergies, or minor tear duct blockages rather than an acute medical crisis. However, if you notice the eye becoming red, your dog squinting, or the discharge turning thick and yellow/green, you should schedule a veterinary appointment promptly as these can be signs of infection or injury.

How Photos Help Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of your dog's eyes is an essential part of the triage process. A high-quality image allows a veterinary professional to see the exact location of the staining, the health of the surrounding skin, and whether the eye itself looks clear or inflamed. It also helps differentiate between common porphyrin staining and more serious issues like corneal ulcers or ocular swelling that might not be as obvious to the untrained eye.

faqs.question: Is tear staining painful for my dog?
faqs.answer: The staining itself is not painful, as it is just a chemical reaction on the fur. However, the underlying cause of the excessive tearing, such as seasonal allergies or ingrown eyelashes, may cause mild irritation or discomfort.

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my dog?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human medications or eye drops on your pet without a direct recommendation from your veterinarian, as some ingredients can cause severe irritation or mask more serious problems.

faqs.question: Why do white dogs have more visible brown staining?
faqs.answer: All dogs produce porphyrins, but the resulting rust-colored oxidation is much more visible against white or light-colored fur. Dogs with darker coats have the same tearing, but the color blends in.
